content: Most innovation failures are not execution failures — they are framing failures. Teams invest enormous energy solving problems with skill and diligence, only to discover they were solving the wrong problem entirely. The discipline of sitting with a problem longer, resisting the pull toward solution, and interrogating whether the question itself is correctly formed is the highest-leverage skill in the innovation toolkit.
implication: Before any ideation session, teams should invest proportionally more time in problem definition than feels comfortable. If the problem statement can be written in thirty seconds, it probably hasn't been examined deeply enough to deserve a solution.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: The 'How Might We' question is not a warm-up exercise or a facilitation formality — it is a precision instrument that determines the entire solution space before a single idea is generated. A narrowly framed HMW question produces incremental answers; a broadly or creatively framed one opens up entirely different solution territories. The quality of the question is a direct ceiling on the quality of the answers.
implication: Treat the construction of the HMW question as a standalone work session, not a preamble. Draft multiple versions, stress-test each one for hidden assumptions, and choose the framing that opens the most generative space rather than the one that feels safest.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Design thinking's most underappreciated step is the one that happens before ideation: deep empathy work that reframes what problem is actually worth solving. Utley emphasizes that d.school methodology is built on the premise that the user's stated problem is rarely the real problem — skilled innovators treat problem discovery as iterative fieldwork, not a preliminary checkbox.
implication: Innovation teams should spend at least as much structured time in problem discovery as in solution generation. The impulse to skip to ideation is a bias toward action that reliably produces elegant solutions to misidentified problems.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Reframing is not a creative luxury — it is a systematic practice with concrete techniques. One of the most powerful is shifting the level of abstraction in the problem statement: zoom out and the problem becomes a different challenge entirely; zoom in and what seemed like a strategic question becomes an operational one. The same underlying situation can generate radically different solution spaces depending solely on how the question is pitched.
implication: When a brainstorm produces only incremental ideas, the first diagnostic question is not 'do we need better people?' but 'is the framing too narrow or too broad?' Rewriting the problem statement at a higher or lower level of abstraction is the fastest way to unlock a new solution space.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley teaches that the instinct to immediately generate solutions upon encountering a problem is the primary enemy of breakthrough innovation. The productive procrastination of remaining in the problem space — deliberately deferring solution-mode — is not laziness but a form of cognitive discipline that allows the problem to reveal its real structure before constraints are imposed.
implication: Build structured delays into innovation processes. Schedule problem-immersion sessions that have an explicit prohibition on solution proposals. The discomfort of sitting with an unsolved problem is often the signal that the reframing work is still incomplete.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on The Power of Productive Procrastination — video

content: A core insight from d.school methodology that Utley repeatedly surfaces is that innovation is not primarily about generating solutions — it is about finding the right problem. Most organizations are systematically over-invested in solution generation and under-invested in problem finding. This imbalance is structural, not accidental: solution generation feels productive and legible; problem finding feels ambiguous and uncomfortable.
implication: Leaders should audit their innovation process for the ratio of time spent in problem exploration versus solution generation. If the ratio heavily favors solution work, the organization is likely operating with a false economy — moving fast toward destinations that may not be worth reaching.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley and Perry Klebahn | Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters | Talks at Google

content: Utley frames the 'Zero to One' problem — the myth that the hardest part of innovation is the first idea — as a framing trap. Teams that romanticize the moment of initial inspiration misunderstand where leverage actually lives. The critical work is not the flash of the first idea but the disciplined refinement of the question that makes the right first idea possible.
implication: Deprioritize the mythology of the eureka moment in your innovation culture. Replace it with systematic question-design rituals that treat problem framing as a team sport requiring iteration, not an individual act of inspiration requiring genius.
source_ref: Zero to One: The Myth That Keeps Individuals, Teams, and Organizations from Breakthrough Innovation — video

content: One of Utley's consistent observations from teaching at Stanford d.school is that most problem statements contain hidden assumptions that silently constrain the solution space. These assumptions are invisible precisely because they feel like facts rather than choices. Making assumptions explicit — writing them down and then asking 'what if this assumption were false?' — is a rapid technique for expanding the range of possible solutions.
implication: Add an assumption-surfacing step to every HMW formulation process. For each problem statement, list every assumption embedded in it, then systematically challenge each one. The most productive creative breakthroughs often come from invalidating an assumption that everyone in the room had unconsciously treated as fixed.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: AI now offers a powerful new tool for problem reframing: teams can ask AI to rewrite the same problem statement from ten different angles, at different levels of abstraction, or from different stakeholder perspectives in seconds. Utley positions this as one of AI's highest-value applications in innovation work — not generating solutions, but multiplying the number of ways a problem can be seen before any solution work begins.
implication: Use AI as a problem-reframing engine before using it as an idea generator. Feed it your current problem statement and ask it to generate fifteen alternative framings, then bring those alternative framings to the human team for discussion. The diversity of framing alone often unlocks solution directions that would never have emerged from the original statement.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI — podcast

content: Utley makes a clear distinction between problems worth solving and problems that are merely visible and urgent. Urgency creates a gravitational pull toward the presenting problem — the symptom — rather than the underlying structure generating the symptom. Skilled innovators develop a discipline of asking 'is this the problem, or is this the evidence of a problem?' before committing resources.
implication: When a problem surfaces in your organization, create a structured pause before solution work begins. Ask the team: is what we're describing the root problem or a symptom? Treating symptoms with sophisticated solutions is resource-intensive and produces recurrence; addressing root causes requires more upfront framing investment but generates durable change.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: The difference between an incremental innovation and a breakthrough innovation is often traceable to the level of reframing that occurred at the question stage. Incremental innovations typically answer the question as given; breakthrough innovations reframe the question itself. Utley cites this pattern as a core insight from studying innovation at scale — the teams that produce genuinely new things almost always changed the question, not just the answer.
implication: When an ideation session is producing only incremental ideas, treat that as diagnostic feedback about the framing, not about the team's creativity. The solution is not to push harder for better answers — it is to return to the problem statement and ask whether a different question would open more generative territory.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley's Stanford d.school instruction is grounded in the principle that design thinking is not primarily a methodology for generating ideas — it is a methodology for finding the right problem. The empathize and define phases that precede ideation are not setup; they are the core intellectual work. The ideation phase is downstream of good problem framing, and the quality of what emerges in ideation is largely predetermined by how rigorously the problem was examined.
implication: Organizations adopting design thinking should resist the temptation to use it primarily as a brainstorming format. The most transformative application of the methodology is in the front end — empathy research and problem definition — where teams are least comfortable spending time and most likely to rush.
source_ref: Stanford Instructor - Meet Jeremy Utley — video

content: Utley teaches that constraints, when properly understood, are not enemies of creativity but framers of it. A problem with no constraints is actually a problem with no useful frame — it produces unconstrained brainstorming that generates low-signal, unactionable ideas. The most generative creative constraints are those that reflect real-world conditions while remaining flexible enough to allow unusual solutions.
implication: When designing a problem statement, make constraints explicit and examine them carefully. Some constraints are real and should be honored; others are assumed and should be challenged. The act of sorting real constraints from assumed ones is itself a form of problem reframing that almost always expands the solution space.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Unconventional Paths to Breakthroughs — video

content: Utley describes the 'Wonder Wander' as a physical practice of problem reframing: taking a poorly-defined problem out into the world and deliberately colliding it with unrelated stimuli to generate new angles on what the problem actually is. The value is not the random stimuli themselves but the cognitive habit of asking 'what does this tell me about my problem?' — a question that breaks the echo chamber of working entirely within one's existing knowledge.
implication: For problems that feel stuck or that keep generating the same class of solutions, prescribe a structured physical excursion before the next ideation session. Give team members the problem statement and instruct them to spend thirty minutes in an unfamiliar environment, actively asking how each thing they encounter reframes or illuminates the problem.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley positions the question 'what does this make me think of?' as a micro-reframing intervention that can be applied at any stage of the innovation process. The distinction between 'what do I think of this?' and 'what does this make me think of?' is not semantic — it is a shift from evaluation mode to association mode. Applied to problem framing, it transforms the analysis of an existing problem statement into generative exploration of adjacent problem territories.
implication: Train facilitators and innovation leads to deploy this question pivot as a standard tool. Whenever a discussion collapses into evaluation of the current framing, redirecting with 'what does this problem make you think of?' can rapidly surface new angles, analogous challenges from other domains, and reframings that break the convergent gravity.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley consistently emphasizes that the most valuable input to the innovation process is not raw ideas — it is correctly identified problems. The innovation pipeline is not an idea funnel; it is a question funnel. Organizations that deliberately curate and refine their problem statements before opening them to ideation produce systematically better output from the same creative talent than organizations that skip directly to idea generation.
implication: Create an explicit problem portfolio alongside your idea portfolio. Invest in maintaining a curated library of well-framed problems — questions that have been stress-tested for assumptions, abstraction level, and scope. A richly populated problem portfolio is a strategic asset that makes every future ideation session more productive.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley and Perry Klebahn | Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters | Talks at Google

content: When Utley demonstrates AI use with executive audiences, one of the most powerful applications he surfaces is using AI to challenge and stress-test a problem framing in real time. By feeding a draft problem statement to an AI and asking it to identify hidden assumptions, suggest alternative framings, and flag scope issues, teams can compress what would otherwise be a multi-hour problem-definition workshop into a rapid iterative exchange that sharpens the question before any human ideation begins.
implication: Integrate AI as a problem-statement critic into the front end of your innovation process. Before distributing a challenge brief to a team, run it through an AI conversation specifically designed to expose its weaknesses. Treat the AI's reframings not as final answers but as prompts for human deliberation about which framing is most generative.
source_ref: Stanford University's Jeremy Utley. AI's brainstorming and problem-solving potential — video

content: Utley draws a sharp line between problems that are well-understood and under-solved versus problems that are well-funded but poorly framed. Many organizations pour resources into the latter, building sophisticated solution systems for a challenge that was never examined rigorously enough to know whether solving it would produce real value. The discipline of asking 'is this the right problem to solve?' before 'how do we solve it?' is disproportionately rare and disproportionately valuable.
implication: Before approving resource allocation to any new innovation initiative, require the proposing team to present not just a solution concept but a documented case for why this is the right problem — including what alternative framings were considered and rejected, and what evidence of real user or market need exists.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Utley notes that the organizational incentive structure typically punishes problem-finding and rewards solution-presenting, which creates a systemic bias toward premature solution generation. Presenting a well-defined problem without a solution attached reads as incompetence in most corporate environments, so people skip the definition work and go straight to proposals. This social dynamic is one of the primary structural causes of innovation failure that is almost never named.
implication: Leaders who want better innovation outcomes need to actively reward and celebrate rigorous problem-framing work, even when it arrives without a solution attached. Create explicit recognition moments for teams that identify a better question, reframe a problem, or discover that the original challenge was pointing in the wrong direction.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on How to Build an AI Culture to Lead for the Future — video

content: One of Utley's recurring teaching moves is to distinguish between the problem as presented and the problem worth solving. Users, clients, and stakeholders present problems through the lens of their existing mental models, which means the stated problem is always filtered through assumptions about what kinds of solutions are possible. The innovation practitioner's job is to look through the stated problem to the underlying need it reflects, then ask whether there is a better way to frame that need as a design challenge.
implication: In client or stakeholder briefings, treat the initial problem statement as raw material for problem-finding, not as a mandate. Develop a standard reframing protocol — a set of questions you ask in every briefing — that systematically surfaces the underlying need behind the stated problem and opens the conversation to alternative framings before any solution work begins.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care?

content: Utley teaches that creative breakthroughs often emerge not from generating more ideas within an existing frame but from discovering that the frame itself was the constraint. This is what distinguishes truly generative innovation from sophisticated optimization — the optimizer accepts the frame and works within it; the innovator questions whether the frame is the real problem. Developing the reflex to challenge the frame before engaging with it is a learnable and teachable skill.
implication: Build a 'frame challenge' step into every innovation sprint before ideation begins. Ask the team: what if the frame we've been given is itself the problem? What frame would open up solution spaces we haven't yet considered? This single question, asked consistently, is one of the highest-return interventions available to innovation facilitators.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Unconventional Paths to Breakthroughs — video

content: Utley observes that the skill of problem framing is rarely explicitly taught in professional education or organizational training, despite being the most leverage-rich skill in the innovation process. Most training focuses on ideation techniques, prototyping methods, and evaluation frameworks — all of which are applied after the problem has been framed. The result is a workforce that is often highly skilled at solving problems but systematically underprepared for the harder work of finding which problem deserves to be solved.
implication: If you are designing innovation capability programs for teams or organizations, allocate significant curriculum time to the explicit teaching of problem-framing and question-design skills. This means practicing the rewriting of problem statements, challenging assumptions, adjusting abstraction levels, and stress-testing HMW questions — not just as theory but as hands-on craft work.
source_ref: AI in Learning Series | Ep 1 | Jeremy Utley | How to spark learning innovation with AI — video

content: Utley's research and teaching experience consistently point to the same upstream bottleneck: teams have more ideation capacity than they use, but that capacity is constrained by the quality of the problem they've been given to work on. A mediocre problem statement caps the ceiling of even the best brainstorming session. Raising the quality of the challenge brief is therefore a higher-leverage investment than improving the ideation process itself.
implication: Professionalize the creation of challenge briefs in your organization. Treat the challenge brief as a deliverable that requires iteration, peer review, and quality standards — not as a task that can be drafted in five minutes before the meeting. The hour invested in a well-crafted challenge brief multiplies the return on every hour the team subsequently spends in ideation.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)
